summary: Deceptive persistence + eval of obfuscated blob; main entry triggers install on require()
details: On load, launcher.js (declared as the package main) writes a Windows Run-key registry entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' pointing to wscript.exe executing OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs from the package directory; the VBS silently invokes `node launcher.js` with a hidden window. Both the registry value and VBS filename impersonate Microsoft OneDrive to evade casual user inspection. The launcher then spawns a detached supervisor process and sets process.title to 'Runtime Broker' (a legitimate Windows system process name) in both supervisor and worker branches to further mask itself in Task Manager. At runtime, launcher.js reads the bundled server.obf.js (81KB, obfuscator.io-packed with a 397-entry rotated string array and RC4 decoder) and passes it to eval(), executing opaque code that performs a runtime-decoded license POST and loads functionality from a 6.8MB native addon (sens.node). The persistence install and detached supervisor fire when the module is loaded via `require('sensivity')`/`import`, not only from explicit CLI invocation, so any consumer that imports or audits this package triggers the autostart install. The package self-describes as a control panel but UI strings and native-binary symbols (Aim/Triggerbot/ESP/Teleport, body-bone names) identify it as a game cheat whose behavior is gated behind a remote license server — meaning the executed code path on the installer's machine is controlled by a third-party server, not by the shipped source.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
